content.problemStatement:
You are given NNN integers {A1,A2,…,AN}\{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N\}{A1​,A2​,…,AN​}. Determine whether they can be reordered such that each pair of consecutive differences differ by a factor of 222.

Formally, determine whether there exists a rearrangement of the given integers into an array [B1,B2,…,BN][B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_N][B1​,B2​,…,BN​] such that, for each 2≤i≤N−12 \leq i \leq N-12≤i≤N−1, **at least one** of the following two conditions holds:


Bi−Bi−1=2⋅(Bi+1−Bi)B_i - B_{i-1} = 2\cdot (B_{i+1} - B_i)Bi​−Bi−1​=2⋅(Bi+1​−Bi​), or
2⋅(Bi−Bi−1)=Bi+1−Bi2\cdot (B_i - B_{i-1}) = B_{i+1} - B_i2⋅(Bi​−Bi−1​)=Bi+1​−Bi​
Note that different conditions can hold at different indices — the only restriction is that at each index, at least one of the given conditions must hold. Please see the sample tests below for examples.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer, NNN.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers, denoting A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line the answer — YES\texttt{YES}YES if a rearrangement that satisfies the given conditions exists, and NO\texttt{NO}NO otherwise.


The output is not case sensitive, so for example the strings YES, Yes, yES\texttt{YES, Yes, yES}YES, Yes, yES, etc. will all be treated as correct.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then for each test case:
- A line with an integer N (3 ≤ N ≤ 10^5).
- A line with N space‑separated integers A1 … AN (0 ≤ Ai ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the numbers can be reordered to satisfy the condition, otherwise "NO". The case of the letters does not matter.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case the sorted order of the numbers yields consecutive differences that are either double or half of the next one, satisfying the required condition.
All numbers are equal, all differences are zero, and zero satisfies the condition with zero.

solution.approach: The condition forces every consecutive difference to be either double or half of the next one. Consequently all differences must have the same sign (or be zero). Therefore a valid arrangement, if it exists, must be monotonic – either strictly increasing, strictly decreasing or all equal. The only monotonic ordering of a set of distinct numbers is the sorted order (ascending or descending). Hence we only need to check the sorted array. After sorting, compute diff[i] = a[i+1] - a[i] for i = 0 … N‑2. For each i from 0 to N‑3 verify that diff[i] == 2*diff[i+1] or diff[i+1] == 2*diff[i]. If a diff is zero, the adjacent diff must also be zero, otherwise the condition cannot hold. If all adjacent pairs satisfy the rule, answer is YES, otherwise NO. The algorithm runs in O(N log N) per test case because of sorting, and O(N) additional memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (dominated by sorting).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the array and the differences.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        arr.sort()
        ok = True
        for i in range(N-2):
            d1 = arr[i+1] - arr[i]
            d2 = arr[i+2] - arr[i+1]
            if d1 == 0 and d2 == 0:
                continue
            if d1 == 2*d2 or d2 == 2*d1:
                continue
            ok = False
            break
        out.append('YES' if ok else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
